0: Towards sustainability: An integrated life cycle environmental-economic insight into cow manure management
Tianzuo Zhang a Yueyang Bai b , Xinying Zhou a , Ziheng Li a , Ziyue Cheng a , Jinglan Hong a,c,*

<list>a Shandong Key Laboratory of Environmental Processes and Health, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao 266237, China 
b Graduate School of Environmental Studies, Nagoya University, Nagoya, Japan 
c Shandong University Climate Change and Health Center, Public Health School, Shandong University, Jinan 250012, China</list>

ARTICLEINI|ABSTRACT Keywords: Life cycle costing Waste utilization Life cycle assessment Milk production Environmental external cost|Waste management signifies an equilibrium between environmental and economic factors. However, a comprehensive understanding of the integrated life cycle environmental-economic performance of waste man- agement activities remains unclear. To facilitate a systematic linkage between the economic and environmental sectors, a regionalized life cycle assessment-based life cycle costing method was developed based on China's actual status quo. The cow manure utilization was set as an entry point to explored long-term environmental- economic performance of milk production under various manure utilization pathways. The results show that trade-offs were observed between internal and external costs as well as various environmental indicators. The choice of waste utilization is the focal point of environmental-economic trade-offs in the cow raising system. The optimal environmental-economic performance was achieved through the manure fertilizer utilization pathway, yielding a remarkable three-fold increase in marginal environmental benefits. Compared with fertilizer utiliza- tion, the manure direct returning to field reduced the carbon footprint by 12% while induced an external cost of $14.3. The wastewater treatment pathway is $ 5.5 lower in internal costs but $ 11.7 higher in external costs than those of fertilizer utilization. Overall, utilizing manure has potential to mitigate the upward trend of carbon footprint and external costs. However, achieving the carbon peak remains a significant challenge. A promising solution is the recycling of straw resources within cropping systems, particularly in hotspot regions (e.g., Inner Mongolia, Heilongjiang, Hebei, and Shandong). A comprehensive analysis of the dynamic interplay between cropping systems and cow raising systems is critical steps towards realizing a carbon-neutral future within the dairy production.
1: Crops and livestock products are the preeminent source of daily calorie requirements and play important roles in the development of human civilization, and their production is undergoing intensification on both input and output sides. The intensified production leads to adverse consequences that jeopardize food security, energy provision, environmental quality, and climate change. Approximately 30% of the global final energy consumption is related to the current food system (FAO, 2023), which is responsible for more than 34% of global green- house gas (GHG) emissions (Crippa et al., 2021). The use of fertilizers and pesticides in agriculture has caused soil and water pollution, which decreases soil productivity, accelerates ecological deterioration, and increases potential hazards to human well-being due to the pollution of

food and drinking water (FAO, 2018). These perils are particularly concerning in emerging economies that lack adequate energy supply and waste management strategies (Xing et al., 2022). In this context, envi- ronmental risks associated with food production systems must be ascertained and mitigated by implementing alternative approaches or improving optimization techniques, particularly in emerging economies. Currently, the primitive production mode is gradually being sup- planted by the industrial mode (Bai et al., 2018). The industrial mode can bridge yield gaps (Meul et al., 2012), boost land productivity (van der Werf et al., 2005), and improve feed utilization (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014). This mode also provides a novel opportunity to exploit large amounts of livestock waste (van Zanten et al., 2014) to mitigate agricultural pollution (Fasake and Dashora, 2021; Xing et al., 2022).

However, livestock raising and its upstream processes imply a contin- uous material flow as well as trade-offs in terms of environmental and economic aspects (Xing et al., 2022). Take crop production system as an example, straw not only offers cows a comfortable and hygienic resting surface (Dimov and Marinov, 2021), thereby boosting their well-being and productivity, but it can also be converted into a nutrient-rich organic fertilizer that promotes crop growth and minimizes waste (Zhuang et al. 2020). Moreover, Zhang et al. (2022b) found that live- stock manure composting modified with additives may have potential in improving environmental performance while increasing profits. How- ever, Tthe industrial mode and waste utilization may impose environ- mental and economic burdens on human populations because of the extended utilization chain. In particular, feedstock collection, anaerobic fermentation, manure fertilizer utilization, and biogas utilization still produce unrecognizable environmental effects that may offset the direct environmental benefits generated by waste resource utilization or even incur new environmental burdens (Humpenöder et al., 2018; Mour- atiadou et al., 2020). The direct economic profit of livestock products may be traded off by enhanced material inputs, overhead, and waste management costs (NDRC, 2022). Thus, the utilization of livestock waste poses multiple environmental and economic trade-offs to the system. A comprehensive understanding of these trade-offs is essential to prevent the secondary transfer of environmental and economic burden.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) can be effectively used to examine the environmental performance of the entire production chain (ISO 14040, 2006). Previous studies have focused on isolated aspects of the livestock raising system, such as GHG emissions (Montes et al., 2013; Wei et al., 2023; Zervas and Tsiplakou, 2012), energy potential (Wang et al., 2018), and pollutant emissions (Liu al., 2020). However, these studies were beset by certain limitations, including inadequate representation of study cases and regions, outdated results, and vague definition of system boundaries (Wei et al., 2023). Moreover, the trade-offs of waste management within the integrated system have not been clearly illus- trated. Life cycle costing (LCC) is another method for assessing life cycle sustainability (Rebitzer et al., 2003). With the increasing emphasis on environmental issues, LCC has become an effective business tool and a socioeconomic assessment method (Hall et al., 2018). LCC can be employed to unveil the potential socioeconomic cost of environmental risks when making sustainable decisions for businesses and public administration (Adell et al., 2009). The integration of LCA and LCC is a main consideration for using LCC as a socioeconomic assessment method. LCA-based LCC method is a robust method for linking envi- ronmental risks to the economic sector (Ilyas et al., 2021). By quanti- fying the costs of environmental impacts, LCA-based LCC method can effectively externalize the environmental risk and make them visible to decision-makers. However, it remains a challenge to obtain systematical and robust insights into socioeconomic sustainability of the composite production system because of the lack of transparent life cycle inventory and systematic LCA-based LCC method. China, one of the most promi- nent emerging economies, is witnessing a surge in milk consumption because of the growing demand for high-quality nutrient intake. In 2021, China's annual per capita milk consumption reached a record high of 14.4kg/person/year (NBS, 2022), which is merely one-fifth of that in developed countries (Zhang et al., 2021a). Between 2015 and 2020, the self-sufficiency rate of milk in China decreased by 9.6% from 2015 to 2020 (DAC, 2021), revealing a vast potential market of dairy products in China. Moving forward, China's per capita milk consumption is ex- pected to grow at an average annual rate of approximately 3%, as will the milk production (OECD-FAO, 2018). This growth will subsequently result in an increase in the current annual production of 400 million tons of cow manure (Ma, 2017). However, cow raising and the manure uti- lization result in various environmental impacts and resource con- sumption (e.g., global warming, acidification, fossil scarcity, and eutrophication) (Chobtang et al., 2016; Meul et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018). Especially, greenhouse gas can be emitted at

all stages of dairy raising, including manure stockpiling, enteric fermentation, manure disposal, and agricultural application. The resulting global warming impacts receives widespread attention (Zhang et al., 2021b). Notably, due to interconnected impact streams, the environmental burden could be transferred when mitigation strategies are employed (Yan et al., 2013). Meanwhile, economic feasibility is the decision-making basis for achieving manure utilization and enhancing environmental performance (Tan et al., 2022). Reasonable manners for manure utilization have potential in improving the environmental per- formance and production profits simultaneously (Zhang et al., 2022b). Therefore, further comprehensive life-cycle environmental-economic assessments on various waste utilization scenarios are required to exhibit the complex interconnection between environmental and eco- nomic parameters. The high-value utilization of dairy manure could potentially serve as a pivotal strategy for advancing sustainable dairy production and facilitating a low-carbon transition within the agricul- tural sector, benefitting both the environment and the economy.

Therefore, this study aims to 1) quantify the environmental and economic performance of milk production using LCA-based LCC method, 2) identify key nodes of environmental-economic nexus, 3) determine the trade-offs among various manure management pathways, and 4) provide an optimizing direction for stakeholders.

By quanti- fying the costs of environmental impacts, LCA-based LCC method can effectively externalize the environmental risk and make them visible to decision-makers. However, it remains a challenge to obtain systematical and robust insights into socioeconomic sustainability of the composite production system because of the lack of transparent life cycle inventory and systematic LCA-based LCC method. China, one of the most promi- nent emerging economies, is witnessing a surge in milk consumption because of the growing demand for high-quality nutrient intake. In 2021, China's annual per capita milk consumption reached a record high of 14.4kg/person/year (NBS, 2022), which is merely one-fifth of that in developed countries (Zhang et al., 2021a). Between 2015 and 2020, the self-sufficiency rate of milk in China decreased by 9.6% from 2015 to 2020 (DAC, 2021), revealing a vast potential market of dairy products in China. Moving forward, China's per capita milk consumption is ex- pected to grow at an average annual rate of approximately 3%, as will the milk production (OECD-FAO, 2018). This growth will subsequently result in an increase in the current annual production of 400 million tons of cow manure (Ma, 2017). However, cow raising and the manure uti- lization result in various environmental impacts and resource con- sumption (e.g., global warming, acidification, fossil scarcity, and eutrophication) (Chobtang et al., 2016; Meul et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018). Especially, greenhouse gas can be emitted at

all stages of dairy raising, including manure stockpiling, enteric fermentation, manure disposal, and agricultural application. The resulting global warming impacts receives widespread attention (Zhang et al., 2021b). Notably, due to interconnected impact streams, the environmental burden could be transferred when mitigation strategies are employed (Yan et al., 2013). Meanwhile, economic feasibility is the decision-making basis for achieving manure utilization and enhancing environmental performance (Tan et al., 2022). Reasonable manners for manure utilization have potential in improving the environmental per- formance and production profits simultaneously (Zhang et al., 2022b). Therefore, further comprehensive life-cycle environmental-economic assessments on various waste utilization scenarios are required to exhibit the complex interconnection between environmental and eco- nomic parameters. The high-value utilization of dairy manure could potentially serve as a pivotal strategy for advancing sustainable dairy production and facilitating a low-carbon transition within the agricul- tural sector, benefitting both the environment and the economy.

Therefore, this study aims to 1) quantify the environmental and economic performance of milk production using LCA-based LCC method, 2) identify key nodes of environmental-economic nexus, 3) determine the trade-offs among various manure management pathways, and 4) provide an optimizing direction for stakeholders.
6: This study adhered to international standards for the LCA research framework (ISO 14040, 2006). The functional unit provides a quanti- tative reference for inventory construction and LCIA outcomes. One ton of raw milk (protein percentage of 3.23%) was selected as a functional unit to ensure comparability and consistency among various studies and techniques. All input and output data were collected according to this functional unit. The system boundary and multiple interfaces of the milk production chain is illustrated in Fig. 1a, involving energy and raw material production system, crop production system, dairy cow rearing system, and waste management system. Detailed data, such as feed production, farmland construction, cow feeding, and cow manure management, were collected in accordance with the defined system boundary.

This study developed seven scenarios based on different waste uti- lization pathways, including traditional model for dried dung cake heating (S1), traditional model for direct field return (S2), industrial model for wastewater treatment (S3), industrial model for fertilizer utilization (biogas for heat) (S4-1), industrial model for fertilizer utili- zation (biogas for electricity) (S4-2), industrial model for energetic utilization (biogas for heat) (S5-1), and industrial model for energetic utilization (biogas for electricity) (S5-2). Detailed information on sce- nario establishment is shown in Addendix I.

8: The LCIA method builds a relationship between the energy produc- tion, crop production, cow raising, and waste management systems and several environmental indicators based on relevant environmental mechanisms (Bulle et al., 2019) (Fig. 1b). Here, LCIA analysis was conducted at the midpoint and endpoint levels by using a regionalized LCIA model, the Shandong University-life cycle impact assessment (SDU-LCIA) model (Appendix II). Then, a regionalized LCA-based LCC model named Shandong University-life cycle costing (SDU-LCC) model was developed to establish the connection between the environmental and economic aspects of milk production (Fig. 1b). In this model, total cost comprises internal cost and external cost. Internal cost directly re- flects the actual market cost of raw material consumption, energy con- sumption, land occupation rent, labor fee, and other management costs. External cost could represent the potential environmental risk needed to

The ecosystem remediation costs in this work were defined as the external costs associated with environmental degradation. In our pre- vious study (Qi et al., 2018), we endeavored to quantify this external cost by using either the ecological compensation policy or willingness- to-pay method. However, these methods were limited by subjective biases or unclear principles of value judgment. Moreover, the external cost of ecosystem remediation was not appropriately represented by a homogenous media remediation cost, as the difficulty and cost of remediating contamination varied remarkably across different media. Herein, we developed a regionalized calculation approach of ecosystem remediation cost considering three types of medium (freshwater, ocean, and soil). The calculation method is shown as Eq. (4).

where C and m refer to specific midpoint categories associated with ecosystem quality and medium types, respectively. The foreground LCI of cow raising system on average industrial raising level in China are shown in Table 1. The foreground LCIs of seven waste management systems are shown in Appendix V due to the space limitations. The LCI of the dairy cow rearing system was developed using the operational data from 16 dairy farms ranging in herd size from 1,000 to 10,000 heads. The cost and benefit data were obtained from the statistical reports (NDRC, 2022) and aggregated based on the yield- weighted approach. The operational data of 16 dairy farms were collected from China's main dairy production areas with the aim of improving the representativeness of the LCI. Then, the LCIs of waste management systems were constructed in line with previous studies and international guidance (Appendix VI), including the basic parameters (e. g., average cow weight and production of cow dung and urine) used by Dong (2019). The nitrogen-related emissions (i.e., N2, NH3, NO, and NO2) were calculated on the basis of the technical guidance by MEE (2014). Carbon-related emissions (i.e., CO2 and CH4) were mainly measured according to the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). Previous studies were used for calculation or validation (Appendix VI). The background LCI support was provided by the Chinese process-based life cycle inventory database (CPLCID which contains internationally peer-reviewed data required for this study, such as maize (Bai et al., 2021), cotton (Zhang et al., 2021c), and wheat (Zhai et al., 2019) production.

The ecosystem remediation costs in this work were defined as the external costs associated with environmental degradation. In our pre- vious study (Qi et al., 2018), we endeavored to quantify this external cost by using either the ecological compensation policy or willingness- to-pay method. However, these methods were limited by subjective biases or unclear principles of value judgment. Moreover, the external cost of ecosystem remediation was not appropriately represented by a homogenous media remediation cost, as the difficulty and cost of remediating contamination varied remarkably across different media. Herein, we developed a regionalized calculation approach of ecosystem remediation cost considering three types of medium (freshwater, ocean, and soil). The calculation method is shown as Eq. (4).

17: 3.1. LCC results of the integrated system

The LCC results of the S1, S2, and S4-1 are shown in Fig. 2, while the other waste utilization scenarios are illustrated in Appendix VII due to the space limitations. The arrow of the line represents the flow of external costs, while the thickness of the line reflects the value of external costs. The total cost (includes internal and external cost) of per ton raw milk production varied from $835.9 to $971.8, including 54%- 63% of internal cost and 36%-47% of external cost. S4-1 (fertilizer utilization and biogas for heat) was identified as the scenario with the optimal total cost, which had the lowest external cost ($312.0) and the second largest internal cost ($523.9). The highest total cost was occu- pied by S1 (dried dung cake heating), which had the highest external cost ($451.5) and low internal cost ($520.3). Compared with S1, S4-1 decreased external cost by $139.5 and incurred an internal cost in- crease of only $3.6. This finding seems to point to the conclusion that the environmental benefits of a longer resource use chain outweigh the in- ternal cost increase. Unexpectedly, S5-1 obtained limited external ben- efits (<$11.5) and an increased internal cost (>$11.5) compared with S2, underscoring the necessity of systematic and integrated environ- mental-economic analysis in addressing waste resource utilization issues.

The ecosystem remediation costs ranged from $106.7 to $197.0 per functional unit, accounting for the largest proportion of external cost (54%-64%) because of the enormous global warming potential (Fig. 2 and Appendix VII). An obvious environmental burden was also found in the freshwater ecotoxicity, particulates formation, and fossil depletion categories at the midpoint level. The external cost of the cow raising system constituted a notable contribution for foreground processes, ranging from 49% (S1) to 71% (S4-1). The external cost of waste utili- zation systems exhibited significant variations, which could be attrib- uted to the different utilization routines. Inappropriate waste management led to high external cost. The waste management system of S1 had the highest external cost ($230.1), which was even higher than that of the cow raising system ($221.4). Although substituting coal-fired heating and fertilizer application may produce environmental benefits, fully offsetting the external cost resulting from direct emissions related to the waste management system remains a challenge. Compared with S1, the external cost was decreased by 26% in S2. The environmental benefits of fertilizer utilization offset 51% of the external cost of the waste management system. Although wastewater treatment in S3 effectively reduces environmental burden, its external costs are higher than S4 or S5-1 due to the neglect of the recycling potential of waste resources. Among the fertilizer utilization (S4-1 and S4-2) and energetic utilization (S5-1 and S5-2) scenarios, S4-1 has the lowest external cost ($90.6) for the waste management systems, followed by S4-2 ($99.0), S5-1 ($101.8), and S5-2 ($135.6). This finding can be partially attrib- uted to the relatively better environmental performance of coal-fired heating compared to fertilizer production. For example, the carbon footprint of producing one ton of fertilizer production can be over 3 times higher than that of coal-fired heating (CPCD, 2023). Therefore, the substitution of chemical fertilizer can provide greater environmental benefits than biogas-fired heating systems. Overall, the industrial model for fertilizer utilization (biogas for heat) exhibits the optimal environ- mental performance. Global warming, freshwater ecotoxicity, and par- ticulate matter formation are the dominant categories at the midpoint level, while ecosystem remediation cost is the dominant category at the endpoint level.

20: The LCC results showed the global warming category of cow raising and waste management systems is the primary contributor to external cost, however, while these results could not directly determine the

carbon footprint of raw milk production. Comparable LCIA results of 16 midpoint categories and related uncertainty results based on the defined functional unit were provided in Appendix VIII. The carbon footprint of producing 1 ton of raw milk varied between 1.38 ton CO2 eq and 2.37 ton CO2 eq due to the use of different waste management technologies. As a result, the carbon footprint for national milk production in 2020 was calculated as a variation range of 38.9 Mt CO2 eq (54.2 Mt CO2 eq-93.1 CO2 eq). The carbon footprint of cow raising system reached 1.54 ton CO2 eq per ton, indicating the carbon footprint decreases by up to 10.4% because of the waste management process. The highest po- tential of reducing carbon footprint was attained by S2 (direct field returning), followed by S4-1 (1.43 ton CO2 eq) and S4-2 (1.44 ton CO2 eq). However, the particulates formation impacts of S4-1 (1.45 kg PM2.5 eq) and S4-2 (1.631 PM2.5 eq) are slightly lower than that of S2 (1.94kg PM2.5 eq). The lowest impact on human health was induced by energetic utilization (S5-1, X 10 DALY), followed by fertilization utilization (S4-1, DALY). For ecosystem quality category, S2 and S4-1 displayed the lowest results. In addition, the lowest resource scarcity potential was attained by fertilizer utilization (S4-1, $ 19.1), followed by S4-2 and S5-1.

In this work, the cow raising system was identified as a significant contributor for all seven scenarios (Fig. 2). Thus, we further identified the environmental contributors of the cow raising system based on the LCIA results. As shown in Fig. 3, artificial feed and maize fodder pro- duction were found to be the most important contributors to the envi- ronmental impact of cow raising system. Moreover, direct emissions pose a visible risk to human health and ecosystem quality. In particular, carbon dioxide (66.0%) and methane (33.0%) emitted from artificial feed and maize fodder production were identified as the main culprits adversely impacting human health and ecosystem quality. In addition, particulate matters emitted from artificial feed production has a po- tential impact on human health. Consumption of crude oil and coal in artificial feed and maize fodder production was a key contributor to the resource scarcity category. Overall, the cow raising and crop production were the essential components for enhancing the environmental- economic co-benefits of the cow raising system. The sensitivity anal- ysis required by ISO 14040 (2006) is presented in Appendix IX.

Moreover, direct emissions pose a visible risk to human health and ecosystem quality. In particular, carbon dioxide (66.0%) and methane (33.0%) emitted from artificial feed and maize fodder production were identified as the main culprits adversely impacting human health and ecosystem quality. In addition, particulate matters emitted from artificial feed production has a po- tential impact on human health. Consumption of crude oil and coal in artificial feed and maize fodder production was a key contributor to the resource scarcity category. Overall, the cow raising and crop production were the essential components for enhancing the environmental- economic co-benefits of the cow raising system. The sensitivity anal- ysis required by ISO 14040 (2006) is presented in Appendix IX.
22: The LCC results showed the global warming category of cow raising and waste management systems is the primary contributor to external cost, however, while these results could not directly determine the

carbon footprint of raw milk production. Comparable LCIA results of 16 midpoint categories and related uncertainty results based on the defined functional unit were provided in Appendix VIII. The carbon footprint of producing 1 ton of raw milk varied between 1.38 ton CO2 eq and 2.37 ton CO2 eq due to the use of different waste management technologies. As a result, the carbon footprint for national milk production in 2020 was calculated as a variation range of 38.9 Mt CO2 eq (54.2 Mt CO2 eq-93.1 CO2 eq). The carbon footprint of cow raising system reached 1.54 ton CO2 eq per ton, indicating the carbon footprint decreases by up to 10.4% because of the waste management process. The highest po- tential of reducing carbon footprint was attained by S2 (direct field returning), followed by S4-1 (1.43 ton CO2 eq) and S4-2 (1.44 ton CO2 eq). However, the particulates formation impacts of S4-1 (1.45 kg PM2.5 eq) and S4-2 (1.631 PM2.5 eq) are slightly lower than that of S2 (1.94kg PM2.5 eq). The lowest impact on human health was induced by energetic utilization (S5-1, X 10 DALY), followed by fertilization utilization (S4-1, DALY). For ecosystem quality category, S2 and S4-1 displayed the lowest results. In addition, the lowest resource scarcity potential was attained by fertilizer utilization (S4-1, $ 19.1), followed by S4-2 and S5-1.

In this work, the cow raising system was identified as a significant contributor for all seven scenarios (Fig. 2). 23: <header>4.1. Identification of trade-offs in the integrated system </header>

This study shows the carbon footprint of direct field return (S2) was 16% lower than that of energetic utilization, whereas the particulates formation potential of direct field return was 65% higher than that of energetic utilization. Dung cake heating (S1) can offset coal consump- tion while increasing particulate emissions and freshwater ecotoxicity potential. Direct wastewater treatment (S3) can mitigate the environ- mental burden but discard high-value substances in cow manure. Similarly, industrial pathways (S4 and S5) can effectively exploit the utilization potential of cow manure while releasing more pollution due to the extended process chain (Fig. 2). Previously, Xing et al. (2022) constructed an integrated crop-livestock-bioenergy system and deter- mined the need to evaluate extensive environmental indicators. Inade- quate environmental categories do not accurately reveal the trade-offs between different utilization options because of the possibility of shift- ing the environmental burden from one impact category to another (Teillard et al., 2016). The world emissions inventory is often applied in the normalization process to generate comparable results for different environmental categories (McClelland et al., 2018). Although the abovementioned approach reduces the effects of subjectivity because of the weighting method, meeting the representation of such as temporal change (Zhang et al., 2022c), spatial variation (Verones et al., 2020), and targeted LCI (Zhang et al., 2023) remains a challenge. In the current study, we employed the constructed regionalized LCA-based LCC model to visualize and externalize the potential environmental impacts, thus

avoiding the subjectivity of the weighting process and meeting the regional status quo. The external cost of the carbon footprint of milk production in China is approximately $ 78.9 per ton GHG emissions, which is almost the same as the costs of carbon capture and storage for coal-fired ($ 78) and gas-fired ($ 89) power generation (Lawrence, 2017). Moreover, the abovementioned value is within the range of carbon sink prices of the afforestation cost method from ¥257.5 (22 $37.3; Chen et al., 2022) to ¥670.3 (~2 $96.9; Huang and Chen, 2016), also within the estimated costs for sequestering in the US from $30 to $90 per ton of CO2 (Kenneth and Robert, 2005). Overall, the LCA-based LCC method developed in this study is robust and stable in externalizing the potential environmental impacts and reflecting the trade-offs be- tween environmental indicators.

Determining the trade-offs between internal and external cost is essential for effective resource conservation and recycling. In the present study, the internal cost of fertilizer utilization (S4) and energetic utili- zation (S5) are higher than those of S2 and S3, which can be explained by pollution emitted from the extended waste treatment chain. Although the in-depth processing results in higher internal cost, the value of waste utilization is fully exploited. In such circumstances, the environmental benefits are greater than the actual cost. Compared with S2, the fertilizer utilization (S4) incurred an internal cost increase of <$4.7 but an external cost reduction of >$14.3, indicating that only a $1 investment in the internal cost of manure fertilizer utilization results in over 3-fold the external environmental benefits. Biomass utilization is considered to be an important measure to achieve sustainable agricultural develop- ment (Wang et al., 2022), especially with the lowest costs of carbon capture and storage (Lawrence, 2017). S4-1 results showed that an in- ternal cost increase of $6.3 can mitigate the carbon footprint of 0.2 ton CO2 eq (approximately $31.5/ton This value is consistent with the cost of CO2 avoided by the fertilizer industry ($27.8/ton CO2 eq) (Lawrence, 2017). The abovementioned results underscore the envi- ronmental and economic feasibility of enhancing the sustainability of milk production via proper manure utilization pathways, and the po- tential of manure utilization should be fully exploited because of its low costs and high carbon storage potential.

28: <header>4.1. Identification of trade-offs in the integrated system </header>

This study shows the carbon footprint of direct field return (S2) was 16% lower than that of energetic utilization, whereas the particulates formation potential of direct field return was 65% higher than that of energetic utilization. Dung cake heating (S1) can offset coal consump- tion while increasing particulate emissions and freshwater ecotoxicity potential. Direct wastewater treatment (S3) can mitigate the environ- mental burden but discard high-value substances in cow manure. Similarly, industrial pathways (S4 and S5) can effectively exploit the utilization potential of cow manure while releasing more pollution due to the extended process chain (Fig. 2). Previously, Xing et al. (2022) constructed an integrated crop-livestock-bioenergy system and deter- mined the need to evaluate extensive environmental indicators. Inade- quate environmental categories do not accurately reveal the trade-offs between different utilization options because of the possibility of shift- ing the environmental burden from one impact category to another (Teillard et al., 2016). The world emissions inventory is often applied in the normalization process to generate comparable results for different environmental categories (McClelland et al., 2018). Although the abovementioned approach reduces the effects of subjectivity because of the weighting method, meeting the representation of such as temporal change (Zhang et al., 2022c), spatial variation (Verones et al., 2020), and targeted LCI (Zhang et al., 2023) remains a challenge. In the current study, we employed the constructed regionalized LCA-based LCC model to visualize and externalize the potential environmental impacts, thus

The external cost of the carbon footprint of milk production in China is approximately $ 78.9 per ton GHG emissions, which is almost the same as the costs of carbon capture and storage for coal-fired ($ 78) and gas-fired ($ 89) power generation (Lawrence, 2017). Moreover, the abovementioned value is within the range of carbon sink prices of the afforestation cost method from ¥257.5 (22 $37.3; Chen et al., 2022) to ¥670.3 (~2 $96.9; Huang and Chen, 2016), also within the estimated costs for sequestering in the US from $30 to $90 per ton of CO2 (Kenneth and Robert, 2005). Overall, the LCA-based LCC method developed in this study is robust and stable in externalizing the potential environmental impacts and reflecting the trade-offs be- tween environmental indicators.

Determining the trade-offs between internal and external cost is essential for effective resource conservation and recycling. In the present study, the internal cost of fertilizer utilization (S4) and energetic utili- zation (S5) are higher than those of S2 and S3, which can be explained by pollution emitted from the extended waste treatment chain. Although the in-depth processing results in higher internal cost, the value of waste utilization is fully exploited. In such circumstances, the environmental benefits are greater than the actual cost. Compared with S2, the fertilizer utilization (S4) incurred an internal cost increase of <$4.7 but an external cost reduction of >$14.3, indicating that only a $1 investment in the internal cost of manure fertilizer utilization results in over 3-fold the external environmental benefits. Biomass utilization is considered to be an important measure to achieve sustainable agricultural develop- ment (Wang et al., 2022), especially with the lowest costs of carbon capture and storage (Lawrence, 2017). S4-1 results showed that an in- ternal cost increase of $6.3 can mitigate the carbon footprint of 0.2 ton CO2 eq (approximately $31.5/ton This value is consistent with the cost of CO2 avoided by the fertilizer industry ($27.8/ton CO2 eq) (Lawrence, 2017). The abovementioned results underscore the envi- ronmental and economic feasibility of enhancing the sustainability of milk production via proper manure utilization pathways, and the po- tential of manure utilization should be fully exploited because of its low costs and high carbon storage potential.
29: <header>4.2. Long-term pathway of carbon neutrality and external cost reduction </header>

The milk production in China in 2022 reached 34.4 million tons which is nearly 5 times that in 2001 (NBS, 2022). Compared with developed countries, the consumption of raw milk per capita in China is still at a low level. To promote the healthy development of the dairy industry, China has been implementing a series of policies since 2013, such as "construction of high-quality milk bases" and "student drinking milk program" (CPC Central Committee, 2022; MOA, 2013). Per-capita

milk consumption is predicted to increase with the growing demand for a healthy diet owing to improved living standards in China. Wang et al. (2021) found a positive correlation value of 0.8 between the growth of dairy consumption and economy. Based on the ARIMA model, the milk production volume is anticipated to reach 116 Mt in 2060, corre- sponding to a per capita milk production of 96 kg (Fig. 4a). This value is significantly lower than the current per capita milk consumption in the United States (226 kg; Our World in Data, 2022), but close to the rec- ommended daily average intake of 300 g stipulated by the Chinese Di- etary Guidelines (CNS, 2022). According to the National Information Center's forecast, China's GDP per capital is anticipated to surpass 3.5 times its 2020 value by the year 2050 (NIC, 2020). Consequently, the volumetric milk consumption in China is projected to escalate to 89 Mt by 2050 (Wang et al., 2021). The OECD-FAO's forecast for China's milk consumption entails a projected average annual growth rate of approximately 3% (OECD-FAO, 2018). The trend of China's milk pro- duction shown in Fig. 4a concurs with the aforementioned forecast. Due to the rapid growth of dairy consumption, the policy choice of milk production will have significant impacts on carbon peak times and the resulting carbon footprints in 2060 (Wei et al., 2022). Thus, it is imperative for China to identify the long-term pathways of carbon neutrality and external cost reduction. At present, three policy direction are as follows: 1) increasing industrial rate of cow raising, 2) promoting cow manure collection, and 3) improving manure utilization. This study predicted the performance of carbon footprint and the external cost of milk production between 2021 and 2060 based on nine settled policy choices (Appendix X). As illustrated in Fig. 4, the carbon footprint of most pathways failed to achieve carbon peaking by 2060, while path- ways T4 (promoted manure collection & no promotion of fertilizer and energetic utilization) and T7 (enhanced manure collection & no pro- motion of fertilizer and energetic utilization) reached the peak value of 69 Mt CO2 eq in 2057. The policy choices can result in a variation of approximately 15 Mt CO2 eq in the carbon footprint in 2060. The highest carbon footprint in 2060 can be attained by T2 (natural increase in manure collection & promoted fertilizer and energetic utilization, 83 Mt CO2 eq), followed by T5 (promoted manure collection & promoted fertilizer and energetic utilization, 79 Mt CO2 eq), T8 (enhanced manure collection & promoted fertilizer and energetic utilization, 79 Mt CO2 eq), and T3 (natural increase in manure collection & promoted fertilizer utilization, 77 Mt CO2 eq). Overall, improving the collection ability of manure resources is the fastest pathway to mitigate the increase in the carbon footprint of milk production (T7, T8, and T9). However, exces- sive manure collection requirements may cause a rebound in carbon emissions due to limitations in manure utilization ability. For example, the carbon footprint of T8 after 2046 is even higher than that of T1 (natural increase in manure collection & no promotion of manure

Fig. 4. Long-term pathway of carbon neutrality and external cost reduction. a) Prediction of milk production in China, b) the carbon footprint of milk production, and c) the external cost of milk production. Detailed pathway establishment is shown in Appendix X.

utilization), highlighting the necessity of the life cycle environmental optimization of milk production, because the carbon reduction potential of biogas utilization hardly offsets the carbon emissions from manure collection and disposal (Appendix VIII). Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) observed a similar phenomenon in which small farms produce a lower level of GHG than large farms because they mainly handle manure daily. The reduced carbon emissions of milk production in 2060 do not signify a low external cost. Instead, all nine pathways show an increased external cost before 2060 (Fig. 4c). Among them, T6 and T9 have the lowest external cost of approximately $ 29 billion for milk production in 2060. The external cost of particulates formation of direct field return was 25% higher than that of fertilizer utilization but 39% higher than that of energetic utilization. Overall, accelerating the construction of large-scale raising farms and promoting fertilizer utilization can reduce both the carbon footprint and the resulting external cost of milk pro- duction. Reasonable policy choices based on comprehensive analysis are
essential for realizing the sustainable milk production.

33: <header>4.3. Utilization potential of waste from crop production system </header>

As depicted in Fig. 4, a peak node on the carbon footprint and external cost before 2060 can hardly be reached by simply promoting industrial raising, manure collection, and manure utilization (Fig. 4). The crop production system serves as the key contributor to the external cost and carbon footprint incurred by the cow raising system (Fig. 3). Crop production systems entail many utilization approaches, such biogas (Mazurkiewicz et al., 2019), bioethanol (Hong et al., 2015), power generation (Xu et al., 2016), biochar (Zhang et al., 2022a; Wang et al., 2022), fertilizer (Yin et al., 2018), and livestock feed (Ouatahar et al., 2021). The soil application of biochar has been recommended as an effective method for mitigating the climate change caused by agri- cultural sources (Xia et al., 2023). Hepburn et al. (2019) proposed that

biochar has promising application prospects because of its low cost and considerable GHG reduction effect. In contrast, Smith et al. (2016) found that technical and financial obstacles hinder the development of biochar production from the pyrolysis of crop straw. Therefore, the environ- mental-economic performance of multiple straw utilization pathways need to be determined in the future. Currently, the overall utilization rate of straw in China reaches 88.1%, over half of which is contributed by fertilizer utilization (MARA, 2022). The external cost of the cow raising system is incurred mainly due to the cultivation of maize and soybean in the crop production system. According to the current straw collection ability and industrial rate, entire fertilizer utilization of cow manure, maize straw, and soybean straw can generate co-benefits of $18.2 billion while mitigating the external cost of national milk pro- duction by 44.2% (Fig. 5). As the maximum utilization potential is concentrated in Inner Mongolia, Heilongjiang, Hebei, and Shandong, these four provinces need to pay more attention to the development of straw utilization industry related to dairy raising.

35: Waste utilization is a key strategy for improving the efficiency of resource utilization and achieving sustainable development. This study constructed a regionalized life cycle assessment-based life cycle costing method that connects production activity, pollution, impact, damages, and economic burden, and used cow manure utilization as an entry point. Results reveal that optimizing the utilization of cow manure as fertilizer can yield significant environmental co-benefits. Specifically, this approach can reduce external costs by over $14.3 while incurring only a marginal internal cost increase of $4.7. In comparison to con- ventional methods, this approach can mitigate the carbon footprint by 0.7 ton CO2 equivalent and decrease the total production cost by $139.5 per ton of milk produced. Nevertheless, it's essential to acknowledge

that alternative pathways, such as manure field return, while resulting in a 3.6% lower carbon footprint, can induce a 7.3% increase in external costs. Likewise, the wastewater treatment pathway demonstrates a 3.8% higher external cost compared to fertilizer utilization, mainly due to the discarding of high-value substances. Policy choice on waste utilization would significantly affect long-term environmental-economy perfor- mance. The carbon footprint of milk production in China may reach 69-83 Mt CO2 eq in 2060. Expanding capacities for fertilizer utilization, increasing mechanization, and enhancing manure collection can effec- tively temper the growth of the overall carbon footprint in Chinese milk production. However, reaching the peak of carbon footprint and external cost remains a formidable challenge. The utilization potential of maize and soybean straw in the provinces of Inner Mongolia, Hei- longjiang, Hebei, and Shandong must be exploited. These insights contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of the complexities and challenges associated with waste utilization and provide valuable guidance for sustainable waste management strategies. Different waste management pathways can lead to different environ- mental and economic trade-offs within the milk production system. These trade-offs are complex and depend on the method of waste management and the environmental and economic conditions of the region. Further research is needed to better understand these trade-offs and to develop strategies for minimizing their negative impacts.
